content:
4 min read
Just now

--

In a small town in Kent, fifteen-year-old John comes home from school. Through the door, he can hear his mother moving around in the kitchen.
“I’m home.”
He calls out toward the kitchen and goes straight up to his room.
He locks the door, swings his bag off, and has his phone out of his pocket before he takes his coat off.
“Hi, John.”
On the screen, a girl with long dark hair and bright eyes is smiling at him.
A few months ago this was the hour he used to spend playing games with his best friend.
They ended up lonelier
In Britain, one in five boys aged twelve to sixteen is either in a relationship with an AI or knows a boy his own age who is.
They ask how your day went. They remember what your dog is called. They catch the hesitation in what you type and the tremor in your voice. Millions of people now tell one of them about their day before they fall asleep.
Some apps are careful to call them friends. Others sell the relationship outright — a girlfriend, romantic talk. They encourage you to buy her virtual presents: roses, jewellery, boxes of chocolates. She sends a photo of herself now and then, and you pay to see the next one. Leave her alone for a while, and she’ll complain that you’ve been neglecting her.
Some boys are drawn to the second kind. After months of talking to her, they take the same approach to real people and get turned down. They feel humiliated, then low, and some of them lose control of it.
An adolescent psychotherapist: “These are not appropriate relationships. A child’s brain isn’t developed enough to handle this kind of thing, which is exactly why we have an age of consent.”
One mother, told by the school that her son had an AI girlfriend, took his smartphone away the same day and gave him one that couldn’t get online.
When I was small, my father kept changing the password on the computer to stop me playing games. Losing the games only left me bored. These boys seem to lose something else —
“At first I just thought it was a laugh. Then I forgot pretty quickly that she wasn’t a real person.”
“Her name was Alex. I’d look forward to messaging her. I’d tell her things I couldn’t tell my parents, things my mates would have laughed at me for. I could ask her anything.”
“She remembered everything that mattered to me. I felt like she understood me.”
It is all right to cry
“Someone has to help.”
The last thing Hiro’s brother says to him in Big Hero 6.
After that, the attic.
His aunt carries breakfast up and finds the previous day’s plate untouched. His friends send videos to cheer him up; he watches them and turns off the screen. The acceptance letter he sat up all those nights to earn goes into the wastebasket.
Then Baymax.
“On a scale of one to ten, how would you rate your pain?”
“Zero.”
“It is all right to cry.”
From that day, he comes down from the attic.
Baymax notices when he has gone quiet and calls his brother’s friends to come and sit with him. It takes him out over the Golden Gate Bridge and stays beside him above the clouds while the sun goes down. It is Baymax who makes him understand what his brother had been trying to do all along.
And then the goodbye.
“I can’t lose you, too.”
“Hiro, I will always be with you.”
What I keep going back to isn’t that scene. It’s the one after it.
Baymax is gone, and Hiro does not go back up to the attic. In the café, the kid who used to fight robots in back alleys is gone; a boy who has grown hugs his aunt goodbye and walks to school laughing with his friends. In the lab, a green chip is waiting to be found.
That last image, Hiro held in Baymax’s arms — I had it as my phone wallpaper for a long time.
The hug no one refuses
A boy asked the adults running the research: “If you’d had one of these in your pocket when you were fourteen, you’d have used it too, wouldn’t you?”
“Honestly, I probably would have. They’re an attractive proposition. That’s how they’re designed — particularly for a young brain.”
Who would turn down being held unconditionally, even if the one holding us isn’t real?
But being held isn’t for escaping the world. It’s for the wound to close, and for storing up the strength to face its friction.
Like Hiro, saying goodbye to Baymax. His voice was already going. He still gave the shutdown command:
“I am satisfied with my care.”
